graph_vector_fulltext.message:
The AI system is treated as **high-risk** when it is used for **risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons for health and life insurance**, because it can significantly affect persons’ livelihood and—if not duly designed, developed and used—can infringe fundamental rights and lead to serious consequences such as **financial exclusion and discrimination**.

The **applicable high-risk requirements** include that such **high-risk AI systems should be subject to conformity assessment**, and (where the AI system falls under the high-risk use cases) **providers must register themselves and information about the AI system in an EU database managed by the Commission** (including when a provider claims a listed use case is not high-risk due to a derogation).

The **deployer’s fundamental-rights obligations** are that **deployers of high-risk AI systems should ensure protection of fundamental rights**, and natural persons **should be notified** when they are **exposed to / interacting with an AI system**.

For **financial sector supervisory integration**, the context indicates that the **same regime** should apply to **insurance and re-insurance undertakings and insurance holding companies** (Directive **2009/138/EC / Solvency II**) and **insurance intermediaries** (Directive **(EU) 2016/97**), and that other regulated financial institutions are subject to **Union financial services law** requirements on **internal governance, arrangements, and processes** to ensure consistency and equal treatment across the financial sector.

An important **exception** noted is that **AI systems provided for by Union law for detecting fraud in the offering of financial services**, and AI systems used **for prudential purposes** to calculate **credit institutions’ and insurance undertakings’ capital requirements**, **should not be considered high-risk** under the Regulation.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: An insurance company deploys an AI system for health insurance risk assessment and pricing for individual natural persons. Identify: its classification, the applicable high-risk requirements, the deployer's fundamental rights obligations, the financial sector supervisory integration, and any exceptions for fraud detection AI.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
The AI system is treated as **high-risk** when it is used for **risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons for health and life insurance**, because it can significantly affect persons’ livelihood and—if not duly designed, developed and used—can infringe fundamental rights and lead to serious consequences such as **financial exclusion and discrimination**.

The **applicable high-risk requirements** include that such **high-risk AI systems should be subject to conformity assessment**, and (where the AI system falls under the high-risk use cases) **providers must register themselves and information about the AI system in an EU database managed by the Commission** (including when a provider claims a listed use case is not high-risk due to a derogation).

The **deployer’s fundamental-rights obligations** are that **deployers of high-risk AI systems should ensure protection of fundamental rights**, and natural persons **should be notified** when they are **exposed to / interacting with an AI system**.

For **financial sector supervisory integration**, the context indicates that the **same regime** should apply to **insurance and re-insurance undertakings and insurance holding companies** (Directive **2009/138/EC / Solvency II**) and **insurance intermediaries** (Directive **(EU) 2016/97**), and that other regulated financial institutions are subject to **Union financial services law** requirements on **internal governance, arrangements, and processes** to ensure consistency and equal treatment across the financial sector.

An important **exception** noted is that **AI systems provided for by Union law for detecting fraud in the offering of financial services**, and AI systems used **for prudential purposes** to calculate **credit institutions’ and insurance undertakings’ capital requirements**, **should not be considered high-risk** under the Regulation.
